header.stance: Treasury yields rose across the curve as resilient demand lifted real rates despite cheaper oil
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The June 30 close broadened the prior session’s front-led repricing into a selloff across the Treasury curve. The 10-year yield finished at 4.44%, up six basis points, as a rise in its real yield to 2.20% accounted for four basis points of the move. The central interpretation changed at the margin: long-duration demand and easing energy pressure no longer insulated intermediate Treasuries from evidence of resilient labor demand, firmer consumer confidence and powerful equity risk appetite. Inflation compensation also rose, so the close carried both a higher real-rate hurdle and a modest restoration of inflation risk.

US data supplied the first transmission channel. May job openings exceeded expectations and edged above April’s revised level, suggesting that labor demand had not weakened decisively. Consumer confidence improved, even though households’ assessment of the job market deteriorated. A Texas services survey paired faster revenue and employment with higher input costs, wages and selling prices. That combination gave the Federal Reserve room to remain restrictive and encouraged renewed expectations of tightening later in the year. Softer house prices and the uneven confidence details prevented the data from becoming a clean acceleration signal.

Global duration delivered a strong counterpoint without rescuing Treasuries. Lower oil helped inflation slow more than expected in Germany and France, reducing the urgency for consecutive ECB increases. Japan moved in the opposite direction: strong demand at a two-year auction coincided with a sharp super-long selloff as fiscal expansion, a deeply weak yen and doubts about the pace of BOJ normalization raised long-run inflation and supply compensation. The resulting cross-market message was disinflation at the energy margin alongside persistent pressure on real rates and sovereign term premium.

Credit remained accessible in aggregate, yet financing outcomes became more selective. Japanese companies completed a record quarter of foreign-currency issuance, and emerging-market international bond sales reached a first-half record. Sable Offshore’s sharply downsized leveraged loan, China’s restrictions on high-cost offshore borrowing and growing concern about the maturity mismatch in AI debt showed where that access was weakest. The close therefore represented a risk-on, higher-real-yield session with functioning primary markets, rather than a generalized withdrawal of liquidity. Its main unresolved issue is whether cheaper energy can keep inflation expectations contained when labor demand, capital spending and asset prices remain firm.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasury losses were broad and concentrated most heavily around the benchmark maturity. The two-year yield rose four basis points to 4.14%, the five-year increased five to 4.19%, the 10-year climbed six to 4.44%, and the 30-year added five to 4.91%. This replaced Monday’s mild bear flattening with a modest bear steepening through 10 years. Policy restraint still mattered at the front, but stronger growth-sensitive pricing and reduced demand for protection extended the adjustment into the belly and long end.

Curve slopes captured that change in leadership. The 2s10s spread widened two basis points to 30, and 2s30s widened one to 77. The five-year remained rich against its wings: the 2s5s10s butterfly held at minus 20. The 2s10s30s butterfly rose three basis points to minus 17 as the 10-year underperformed the average of the front end and long bond. Those moves were measured, yet they show that the session was more than a mechanical reversal in the two-year sector.

The longer comparison still separated near-term policy pressure from distant duration relief. Over one month, the two-year yield was 16 basis points higher and the five-year six higher, whereas the 10-year was one basis point lower and the 30-year eight lower. June’s oil retreat and lower inflation expectations preserved gains at longer maturities, even as the hawkish Fed shift lifted the front. Tuesday’s selloff narrowed that divergence without eliminating it. Investors raised the cost of holding duration for the day while retaining a substantially different monthly judgment about the policy path and the far-end clearing rate.

Inflation-linked markets divided the six-basis-point rise in the nominal 10-year yield between real and inflation components. The 10-year real yield rose four basis points to 2.20%, and the breakeven increased two to 2.24%. Five-year real yields gained three basis points to 1.93%, seven-year real yields rose four to 2.06%, and 20- and 30-year real yields each added five, to 2.54% and 2.73%. The parallel increase in real compensation indicates that the nominal selloff was rooted in a higher growth and policy hurdle, with a smaller contribution from inflation risk.

May job openings provided the clearest same-session catalyst. Available positions rose to 7.59 million, slightly above April’s revised level and above expectations. The release suggested stable labor demand after the recent pickup in payroll growth and weighed on Treasuries. Conference Board confidence also edged higher in June, reinforcing resilience in aggregate demand. Its labor-market details were less reassuring: consumers’ assessment of employment conditions deteriorated and they expected little improvement. The data therefore weakened the case for imminent policy relief without establishing a uniformly stronger labor market.

Regional and housing data kept the Fed signal two-sided. The Dallas Fed’s Texas services revenue index rose to 9.8 from 5.0, employment to 8.1 from minus 3.2, and general business activity to 2.9 from minus 7.7. Input prices climbed to 36.5 from 29.7, wages and benefits to 15.3 from 6.2, and selling prices to 7.7 from 5.0. Against that firmer activity-and-cost mix, the FHFA house-price index fell 0.1% in April and annual appreciation slowed to 2.0%, tempering the housing-inflation and collateral signal.

Expectations for at least one Fed increase in the second half had persisted through the collapse in oil from its wartime peak, and Tuesday’s releases strengthened that restrictive interpretation. The curve did not price a simple revival of the energy shock: crude fell on the day and most of the 10-year move came from real yield. The combination instead suggested that policy may need to stay tight because labor demand and spending have remained resilient, with AI-related investment adding another possible demand and inflation channel. Softer housing and weaker consumer labor perceptions remain credible competing evidence.

The bill curve showed localized quarter-end pressure rather than a uniform cash squeeze. The four-week coupon-equivalent yield fell two basis points to 3.67%, six weeks rose five to 3.73%, and eight weeks held at 3.73%. Thirteen- and 17-week yields were unchanged at 3.83% and 3.88%, while 26 and 52 weeks each added one basis point, to 3.98% and 3.97%. The sharpest daily move sat at six weeks, where new supply and the holiday funding window concentrated attention.

Treasury’s $80.000 billion 42-day bill auction stopped at a 3.655% high rate with a 2.71 bid-to-cover ratio. Indirect bidders took 69.01% of accepted competitive tenders, direct bidders 4.96%, and dealers 26.03%; 54.61% was allotted at the high. The July 2 issue and settlement date keeps Tuesday’s bidding outcome separate from the coming cash transfer. The sale established absorption across quarter-end, though the simultaneous rise in the six-week cash yield left the funding signal less uniformly easy than the shortest bill suggested.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Listed credit returns reflected the Treasury selloff more clearly in higher-grade duration than in lower-quality risk. LQD fell 0.5743%, while HYG declined 0.0500% and JNK slipped 0.0311%. Strong equities and lower volatility helped speculative-grade funds stay near flat, even as the rise in benchmark yields pulled down the longer-duration investment-grade fund. The pattern described stable broad risk appetite with a duration drag, leaving issuer terms and funding structures to reveal where credit access was genuinely constrained.

Japanese issuers demonstrated exceptional access to dollar markets. Mizuho Financial Group and Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group priced almost $10 billion of debt at the start of the week, lifting quarterly foreign-currency issuance by Japanese companies to about $54 billion. That total was more than 80% above a year earlier and the first quarter above $50 billion in data back to 1999. Panasonic began another dollar offering Tuesday. A weak yen increases the domestic proceeds available after swapping funds back, while higher local rates make offshore financing more relevant for acquisitions and expansion.

Sable Offshore exposed the opposite end of the absorption spectrum. Limited demand for a 15% fixed-rate leveraged loan led its size to be cut from an original $1 billion to $675 million, despite an offer price of 97 cents and a short two-and-a-half-year maturity. The company also sought $400 million through equity and convertible notes, with the converts offering a coupon of as much as 6.5% and a 30% to 35% conversion premium. Its shares fell as much as 55%, showing the dilution and refinancing cost imposed by a near-term liability.

The Sable financing was intended to repay an Exxon Mobil term loan whose maturity had already required a $30 million extension payment; the revised extension was set to run through July 31. Amortization sweeteners could reduce the loan balance over its life, but litigation around the company’s restarted California offshore operations still complicated cash-flow visibility. Strong general demand in leveraged finance did not translate into full absorption for a borrower combining execution risk, compressed timing and an unusually high coupon.

China tightened the boundary around expensive offshore refinancing. The National Development and Reform Commission asked selected bankers to avoid underwriting offshore yuan bonds yielding above 4% and dollar bonds above 5%, particularly for local-government financing vehicles. LGFVs had about $117 billion of offshore bonds outstanding, and Chinese companies faced roughly $71 billion of maturities during the rest of 2026, followed by a wall above $130 billion next year. Some quotas had been reduced by 10% to 20%, forcing at least one state-owned borrower toward costlier short-term bank funding.

Private markets showed a related liquidity fault line. Len Tannenbaum was seeking capital for a new fund as redemption requests rose across major alternative managers and liquidity drained from parts of the $1.8 trillion private-credit market. He identified smaller software companies as especially vulnerable to refinancing trouble within a year, even as lower-middle-market borrowers with real cash flow retained access. The divergence suggests that a broad pool of private capital can coexist with tighter covenants and higher spreads where AI disruption has weakened earnings visibility.

AI financing added a duration mismatch to the credit story. IMF capital-markets director Tobias Adrian focused on hyperscalers issuing medium- and long-term debt to fund chips and other physical assets that may become obsolete much faster. Strong profitability and earnings delivery have so far distinguished the boom from a generalized valuation bubble. The credit consequence is still material: record technology equity performance can support financing today while large future capital needs increase supply and leave long-dated creditors exposed if monetization disappoints.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

European inflation data strengthened the energy-disinflation channel. German inflation slowed to 2.4% in June from 2.7%, below the 2.5% median estimate. France fell to 2.0% from 2.8%, against an expected 2.3%, and Italy moderated to 3.1% when economists had expected no change. Spain’s earlier 3.6% reading remained the main national counterexample. The breadth of the declines reduced the immediate need for another ECB increase and helped explain why lower oil did not generate a larger rise in US breakevens.

ECB officials kept the policy consequence conditional on pass-through. Martins Kazaks said the urgency of consecutive moves had decreased significantly after peace efforts lowered energy prices, while Olli Rehn saw no major second-round effects and described inflation expectations as anchored. Chief Economist Philip Lane remained focused on how four months of higher energy costs might enter food and services. The June rate increase, the first since 2023, left the policy rate at 2.25%; softer national prints shifted the debate toward patience without resolving the pipeline risk.

The German labor release reinforced the case for patience. Seasonally adjusted unemployment fell by only 1,000 in June, and the BA-X labor-demand index held at 103. Social-insurance employment was 71,000 below a year earlier in the latest estimate. Weak labor demand limited the scope for energy costs to create a wage-price loop, even though headline inflation remained above target. European duration therefore received support from both lower current energy inflation and a subdued labor backdrop, with services and food pass-through still the limiting condition.

UK data offered a similar split between resilient output and weaker household demand. First-quarter real GDP growth was unrevised at 0.6%, led by a 0.8% rise in services. Real household disposable income per head fell 0.8%, and the saving ratio dropped to 8.9% from 9.6%. The underlying current-account deficit narrowed to £15.1 billion, or 1.9% of GDP, as a smaller primary-income deficit outweighed a wider trade gap. Growth prevented a pure duration rally, while household income and external-balance improvement reduced separate gilt risk channels.

Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey emphasized that lower oil had reopened policy flexibility. Before the war, pricing for two reductions from the 3.75% policy rate had been reasonable in his view. Crude had since fallen below $80 after exceeding $110, below the path embedded in the BOE’s April forecast. Bailey still expected inflation to rise from 2.8% to around 3.2% later in the year, yet he stressed weak growth and the medium-term horizon. Gilts consequently faced less urgency from energy without receiving a clear near-term easing commitment.

Japan supplied the session’s strongest sovereign-duration counterforce. The 30-year JGB yield rose 11 basis points to 3.94%, and the 20-year climbed nine to 3.64%. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s plan to mobilize more than ¥370 trillion of public and private investment through fiscal 2040 raised concern about future supply and fiscal sustainability. USD/JPY moving above 162 intensified the pressure by highlighting imported inflation and doubts that a government preference for easy policy would allow the BOJ to normalize quickly enough.

The front end told a different Japanese story. The two-year auction drew a 4.82 bid-to-cover ratio, up from 3.70 at the previous sale and above the 3.74 twelve-month average. Its tail narrowed to 0.005 from 0.02, and the two-year yield fell 1.5 basis points after the result. Demand reflected elevated front-end yields and expectations of a cautious BOJ. That strength sharpened the curve split because it did little to resolve long-end fiscal and inflation risk.

Domestic buyer capacity offered a partial long-end stabilizer. Meiji Yasuda planned more than ¥2 trillion of super-long JGB purchases in the fiscal year ending March 2027, double its earlier plan, after the rise in yields. A new BOJ board member appointed by Takaichi also said an inflationary norm was not yet firmly established, reinforcing expectations of gradual normalization. After the New York close, the IMF separately urged New Zealand’s central bank to lift its 2.25% rate toward a neutral level centered near 3% by year-end, adding a later Asia-Pacific tightening signal for the next global session.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Broad emerging-market debt weakened as US real yields rose. EMB fell 0.3925%, while the Dollar Index was nearly flat on the day. The aggregate move was mild relative to the Treasury selloff, and primary access remained exceptionally strong. Emerging-market sovereigns and companies sold a record $450 billion of dollar- and euro-denominated bonds in the first half, 15% more than a year earlier, as spreads over Treasuries narrowed to 154 basis points, their tightest since 2007.

That issuance strength creates a demanding valuation backdrop. Mexico, Saudi Arabia, South Korea, Poland, Turkey and Brazil each raised more than $10 billion, and EM dollar debt returned 2.1% during the first half, three times the Treasury gain. A large global buyer base helped borrowers bring forward funding during tight spreads. Tuesday’s rise in US yields shows the vulnerability on the other side: debt-service costs and secondary performance can deteriorate even when the broad dollar index is calm.

Ethiopia made tangible progress from the legal and coordination stalemate visible at prior closes. A committee representing about 45% of its 2024 noteholders confirmed a tentative agreement on key restructuring terms, and the defaulted securities extended gains to 109.17 cents. The agreement softened the immediate enforcement risk following the 2023 missed coupon. Creditors’ criticism of the IMF’s dual role as senior lender and debt-sustainability arbiter kept Common Framework governance unresolved, so better recovery expectations did not close the process-risk question.

Local policy credibility continued to draw capital into selected Asian sovereigns. Foreign investors bought a net $1.2 billion of Indonesian government bonds through June 26, the strongest monthly pace in more than a year, after Bank Indonesia tightened unexpectedly to support the rupiah. The 10-year yield stood at 7.12%, down from 7.47% earlier in June. India received a record $4.2 billion into index-eligible government debt as lower oil eased its import bill, though a weak monsoon remained a potential food-inflation constraint.

Latin American markets rewarded decisive policy action and disinflation selectively. Colombia raised its policy rate by 75 basis points to 12% after a court ended the government’s ability to obstruct central-bank meetings; bonds and the currency rallied as the result exceeded most forecasts. Poland’s inflation fell to 2.5% from 3.1%, below the 2.7% median estimate, sending its benchmark 10-year yield to the lowest since early March even as the zloty weakened. Chile’s one-year breakeven had dropped more than 100 basis points over the month to 2.19%, concentrating easing expectations at the short end.

South Africa remained on the inflation-sensitive side of that divide. Two-year-ahead inflation expectations rose to 3.9% from 3.6%, above the central bank’s 3% target, and forward-rate agreements priced an 88% chance of a quarter-point increase. The contrast with Poland and Chile shows how the retreat in global oil has not delivered a uniform local-rate outcome. Domestic expectations, food risks and institutional reaction functions still determine whether cheaper energy becomes lower sovereign funding costs.

China’s official June surveys supplied a mixed regional demand signal. Manufacturing returned to expansion at 50.3, with production at 51.4 and new orders at 51.2, but employment fell to 48.5 and the small-firm index to 48.2. Non-manufacturing activity held at 50.2, while construction remained in contraction at 49.0. The figures supported commodity and export demand at the margin without erasing stress among smaller borrowers, which reinforces the refinancing concern created by tighter offshore issuance rules for LGFVs.

A later post-close account added Venezuela to the debt-management risk list. Twin earthquakes had caused an estimated $6.7 billion of direct damage and bonds were falling as investors weighed the fiscal cost against an already difficult restructuring. Its timing prevents that development from explaining the completed US session. It nevertheless adds a next-session sovereign loss-absorption problem in which disaster spending, scarce external liquidity and existing creditor negotiations interact.

## Cross asset

Equities reinforced the rise in real yields and the reduction in immediate haven demand. The S&P 500 gained 0.7920%, the Nasdaq 100 rose 1.6846%, and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index jumped 3.9191%. The VIX fell 6.7989% to 16.45. The leadership was concentrated in technology and chips, but the broad advance still supported the interpretation that labor resilience was translating into earnings confidence rather than an imminent growth break.

Energy complicated that risk-on message in a duration-friendly direction. WTI fell 1.7668% to $69.50 and Brent slipped 0.3144% to $72.92 as hopes for a durable US-Iran settlement reduced the physical-supply premium. Both contracts were down more than 20% over one month. The daily and monthly declines helped European inflation cool and limited the rise in US breakevens, yet they were insufficient to offset the real-yield pressure generated by labor data and strong asset prices.

The dollar and gold showed little demand for systemic protection. The Dollar Index rose only 0.0791% to 101.19, leaving the daily external-liquidity impulse small even though it remained 2.3051% higher over one month. Gold was almost unchanged at $4,022.90 and stood 11.7882% lower over the month. Stable bullion alongside falling volatility and rising equities made the Treasury selloff look more like a growth-and-policy repricing than a renewed geopolitical inflation shock.

Technology’s cross-asset signal remained internally tense. A record quarterly semiconductor advance supported equities, hiring expectations and capital spending, all of which raise the real-rate floor. The same investment cycle requires large and potentially long-dated financing for assets exposed to rapid obsolescence. Equity strength can therefore coexist with rising Treasury yields and greater creditor scrutiny. Lower oil eased the nominal inflation channel, but it did not settle whether AI expenditure will sustain productivity and earnings strongly enough to service the debt created around it.

## Forward Market Significance

The US labor, activity and price sequence on July 1 and July 2 is the nearest test of Tuesday’s real-yield selloff. ADP employment arrives at 8:15 a.m. ET Wednesday, followed by final manufacturing PMI at 9:45 and ISM manufacturing plus construction spending at 10:00; the holiday-advanced payroll report and initial claims follow at 8:30 Thursday. Stable or stronger hiring, firm wages and another elevated input-price reading would confirm that the JOLTS and Texas services signals describe broad resilience, transmitting through two- to 10-year real yields and expectations of further Fed restraint. Weaker employment paired with softer orders and prices would challenge that interpretation and restore the prior belly-duration relief. Thursday’s 2:00 p.m. early close concentrates the reaction in a shorter liquidity window, so depth and persistence of the curve move matter alongside the initial release response.

Euro-area flash inflation at 11:00 a.m. CEST on July 1 determines whether national disinflation has become an area-wide policy signal. A broad slowdown from May’s 3.2% headline rate, especially alongside contained services inflation and oil holding near its lower range, would confirm that France, Germany and Italy are capturing a genuine retreat in the energy shock. The sensitive channels are front-end Bund yields, euro inflation compensation and Treasury breakevens, with lower imported-cost pressure also helping oil-importing EM sovereigns. Sticky services, a rebound in energy or renewed disruption around the US-Iran ceasefire would reopen the pipeline concern emphasized by Lane and Nagel, challenge the reduced urgency expressed by Kazaks and Rehn, and raise the global policy floor even if current crude prices remain well below their wartime peak.

Sovereign absorption shifts to Japan after the US quarter turn. The new roughly ¥2.6 trillion 10-year JGB auction is due July 2, followed by an approximately ¥600 billion 30-year sale on July 7; US 17-week bills on July 1 and the paired $85 billion four- and eight-week auctions on July 2 add a parallel cash-demand check around the Independence Day schedule. Strong end-investor participation and stable long JGB yields would show that Tuesday’s two-year demand can extend beyond the front end, challenging the view that fiscal expansion and yen weakness require a persistently steeper Japanese curve. Weak demand or renewed super-long cheapening would confirm a higher global term-premium channel through Japanese portfolio allocation. In the US, orderly July 2 settlement and stable bill yields would resolve the localized six-week pressure; abrupt cheapening or heavier dealer absorption would reopen the balance-sheet constraint and threaten coupon-market liquidity during the shortened session.

section_summaries.market_read: Duration now faces a higher real-rate hurdle even as cheaper energy limits inflation pressure.
section_summaries.next_check: Whether energy disinflation can persist alongside firm labor demand and asset prices remains decisive.
section_summaries.summary: The Treasury selloff broadened as resilient labor demand, improving confidence and strong risk appetite overcame the duration support from lower energy costs. Higher real yields supplied most of the pressure, while firmer inflation compensation, selective credit access and mixed housing signals kept the close from becoming a simple growth-acceleration story.

section_summaries.market_read: The curve repriced a firmer growth and policy path, with intermediate duration bearing the strain.
section_summaries.next_check: Labor, wage and input-price data must confirm resilience before the real-yield move can extend.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Losses spread across Treasury maturities, led by the benchmark sector, as job openings and firmer services activity weakened the case for near-term policy relief. Real yields drove most of the nominal move, while softer housing, mixed labor perceptions and localized bill pressure prevented a uniformly hawkish reading.

section_summaries.market_read: Broad liquidity is intact, but execution risk and weak cash-flow visibility sharply raise borrower costs.
section_summaries.next_check: Refinancing absorption for stressed borrowers will show whether selective strain is spreading.
section_summaries.summary: Credit remained broadly open, but financing quality mattered more as higher-grade duration lagged riskier funds. Record Japanese and emerging-market issuance contrasted with Sable’s downsized loan, tighter Chinese offshore borrowing limits, private-credit refinancing stress and the maturity mismatch embedded in AI investment.

section_summaries.market_read: Global duration support is uneven because Japan’s term premium offsets European disinflation.
section_summaries.next_check: Services pass-through and long-end Japanese auction demand are the key tests of that divergence.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: European inflation and labor data supported policy patience as cheaper energy reduced near-term tightening urgency, while UK output resilience prevented a clean duration rally. Japan diverged sharply: strong front-end auction demand coexisted with super-long weakness driven by fiscal expansion, yen depreciation and slow normalization.

section_summaries.market_read: Tight valuations leave EM performance increasingly dependent on domestic credibility and funding access.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether higher US yields disrupt new issuance or expose weaker sovereign refinancing plans.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: Emerging-market debt absorbed higher US real yields with only modest aggregate weakness as record issuance and a calm dollar preserved access. Country outcomes remained policy-specific: restructuring progress, credible tightening and lower inflation drew support, while food risks, offshore refinancing limits and disaster costs sustained debt-management pressure.

section_summaries.market_read: Risk assets validated a growth repricing, while cheaper oil contained the nominal inflation channel.
section_summaries.next_check: The durability of technology earnings and capital spending will determine whether debt remains serviceable.
section_summaries.summary: Equity gains and falling volatility reduced haven demand and reinforced the real-yield selloff, with technology leadership signaling resilient investment. Lower crude restrained breakevens and the dollar and gold offered little systemic-stress signal, but AI financing still paired strong earnings optimism with long-dated creditor risk.

section_summaries.market_read: The next sessions can separate durable policy repricing from a thin-liquidity extension.
section_summaries.next_check: Track persistence across real yields, inflation compensation and auction demand after each catalyst.
section_summaries.summary: Near-term data and auctions will determine whether the real-yield selloff, energy-led disinflation and Japanese curve steepening persist. US labor and prices test the restrictive-policy case, euro-area inflation tests global pipeline pressure, and Japanese sovereign supply tests whether front-end demand can extend to long duration.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.2% (+4 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.24% (+2 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 30 bp (+2 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 77 bp (+1 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Treasury selloff broadens as resilient demand lifts real yields across the curve
overall_summary.session_narrative: At the completed New York close, Treasury yields were higher across maturities, with the benchmark sector leading a modest bear steepening. Job openings, firmer confidence and stronger Texas services activity reinforced expectations that policy may remain restrictive, while buoyant equities reduced haven demand. Cheaper energy capped the inflation impulse, and softer housing and weaker consumer labor perceptions kept the growth signal from becoming uniformly strong.
overall_summary.summary: The Treasury selloff spread from the front end to the belly and long end as labor resilience, consumer confidence and equity strength raised the real-rate hurdle. Lower oil restrained inflation pressure, while accessible primary markets kept the session risk-on rather than liquidity-driven.

watch_events.market_implication: Confirmation pressures two- to 10-year real yields; broad weakness restores belly relief.
watch_events.market_trigger: Stable or stronger hiring with firm wages and elevated input prices confirms resilience.
watch_events.title: US labor, activity and price sequence
watch_events.watch_line: Hiring, wages, orders and input prices will test whether broad resilience persists.

watch_events.market_implication: Confirmation supports front-end Bunds and lower breakevens; stickiness lifts the global policy floor.
watch_events.market_trigger: A broad slowdown with contained services and stable oil confirms lower pipeline pressure.
watch_events.title: Euro-area flash inflation
watch_events.watch_line: Headline, services and energy components will show whether national disinflation is broadening.

watch_events.market_implication: Weak demand would lift Japanese term premium and threaten US coupon-market liquidity.
watch_events.market_trigger: Strong end-investor bids and stable bill yields confirm orderly demand beyond the front end.
watch_events.title: Japan and US sovereign absorption
watch_events.watch_line: Long JGB auctions and holiday-window US bills will test sovereign cash absorption.
